questions.question: What is the Compound Components pattern primarily used for?
options: Using multiple CSS classes on one element
Sharing implicit state between parent and child components via Context
Reducing the number of props passed to a component
Combining multiple custom hooks
questions.answer: Sharing implicit state between parent and child components via Context
questions.explanation: Compound components (e.g., Select + Select.Option) use Context to share state without prop drilling, creating an expressive and flexible component API.

questions.question: What is the primary use of React.lazy() and Suspense?
options: Delaying component rendering for animations
Code splitting — loading components only when needed to reduce initial bundle size
Deferring state updates until after paint
Handling network errors in components
questions.answer: Code splitting — loading components only when needed to reduce initial bundle size
questions.explanation: React.lazy() dynamically imports a component, and Suspense shows a fallback while it loads. This reduces initial JavaScript payload by only loading code when the route or component is needed.

questions.question: What is a feature-based folder structure?
options: Organizing files by type: all components together, all hooks together
Organizing files by domain feature, co-locating related components, hooks, and API code
Separating frontend and backend code into separate folders
Creating one folder per developer
questions.answer: Organizing files by domain feature, co-locating related components, hooks, and API code
questions.explanation: Feature-based structure (e.g., features/auth/) groups all related files together, making it easier to delete, move, or test an entire feature as a unit.

questions.question: Why should Error Boundary components be used in React applications?
options: They speed up rendering
They catch JavaScript errors in the component tree and prevent the entire app from crashing
They handle network errors from fetch calls
They validate prop types at runtime
questions.answer: They catch JavaScript errors in the component tree and prevent the entire app from crashing
questions.explanation: Error Boundaries wrap subtrees and show a fallback UI when any child component throws an error during rendering, preventing the error from taking down the whole application.

questions.question: What problem does the Provider pattern solve when combining multiple React Providers?
options: It prevents context value updates
It eliminates deeply nested provider trees in the app entry point
It merges multiple contexts into one
It prevents unnecessary re-renders
questions.answer: It eliminates deeply nested provider trees in the app entry point
questions.explanation: When you have many providers (Theme, Auth, QueryClient, etc.), nesting them manually creates deeply indented code. The ComposeProviders utility flattens this into a clean array.
